source: Learning bash with Rocky - Introduction: Generalities: the shell and what a script is

options: The command interpreter of Linux -- a binary layered on the kernel, not part of it
questions.why: The shell parses the user's commands and has the system execute them; it is a layer on top of the kernel, not part of it.
bash is the Bourne-Again shell; ksh (Korn) and csh (C shell) are other examples.

questions.q: Which shell is present by default in most (all) Linux distributions?
questions.why: bash ships by default nearly everywhere, known for its practical, user-friendly features.

questions.q: As a basic programming language, the shell allows...
options: Variables, conditional execution of commands, and repetition of commands
questions.why: The shell supports variables, conditional execution, and repetition (loops).

options: A plain text file of commands, with no formatting (bold, italics, etc.)
questions.why: It is just a text file; made executable, the shell reads it sequentially and runs each command.

questions.q: Which are two ways to run a shell script?
options: Make it executable and run ./script, or pass its name to the bash binary
Only while logged in as root
questions.why: Either bash script.sh, or chmod +x then ./script.sh.

questions.q: When the shell hits an error partway through a script, by default it...
options: Displays a message identifying the problem but continues executing
questions.why: By default it reports the error and carries on; there are mechanisms to stop on error instead.

questions.q: According to the book, what makes a good script?
questions.why: A good script is reliable (even when misused), commented, readable (indented), and portable.
